39: A direct revelation: he awoke and rebuked the wind and said to the sea, Peace! Be still. The calming of the storm is the exercise of the divine sovereignty over the waters that the OT declares belongs to YHWH alone (Psalms 107:29, Job 9:8). Jesus exercises this sovereignty in his own person, not by prayer or petition to God, but by command: his word silences the storm. The disciples' terrified question (who is this that even the wind and sea obey him?) is the question that Ps 107 answers — YHWH, who stills the storm. Mark presents Jesus as the one doing what the Psalm says only YHWH does.
1: Teaching from a boat on the lake — the boat becomes a pulpit, echoing the divine word that goes out over the waters (Genesis 1:2, Ps 29). The crowd on the shore while Jesus teaches from the water enacts the separation and address that characterizes the prophets’ ministry; Jesus speaks from liminal space appropriate to the one who crosses all boundaries.
2: “He taught them many things by parables” — the parabolic method is the form of speech appropriate to the hiddenness of the incarnation. The kingdom comes disguised in the ordinary (seed, lamp, tree), as the King comes disguised in flesh. Those who have eyes to see recognize the extraordinary within the ordinary.
3: “Listen!” (Akouete) — the parable begins with the Shema imperative (Deuteronomy 6:4). Jesus’s summons to hear is the summons of Israel’s covenant God who demands the undivided attention of the whole person. The parable is not entertainment but a call to the kind of hearing that determines eschatological fate.
4: Seed on the path — eaten by birds before it can take root. The hardened path represents a heart closed by habitual resistance. The birds that devour the unprotected word will recur in Mark 4:32 (the mustard tree shelters birds) — the same creatures appear in both judgment and hospitality imagery.
5: Rocky ground with shallow soil — quick growth is a false sign; the absence of deep roots (depth of relationship with Jesus) means the plant cannot survive the heat of opposition. Superficial enthusiasm for Jesus is not saving faith.
6: “When the sun came up, the plants were scorched” — the sun that gives life to deep-rooted plants destroys the shallow. Suffering and the demands of discipleship reveal whether faith is rooted in Christ or in the benefits of his presence. The disciples’ later failures (Mark 14:50, Mark 14:66-72) will illustrate this soil.
7: Thorns choke the growing plant — the desires for other things (Mark 4:19) are structurally incompatible with the word taking hold. The gospel requires the whole person; it does not fit alongside competing ultimate concerns. This soil anticipates the rich young man (Mark 10:22) who goes away sad because his possessions hold him.
8: Good soil produces thirty, sixty, a hundred times what was sown — the yield is eschatological surplus, far beyond normal Palestinian harvests. The multiplication recalls the feeding miracles (Mark 6:30-44; Mark 8:1-10) where Jesus multiplies loaves beyond calculation. The harvest points forward to the resurrection: from one grain dying, an incalculable life.
9: “Whoever has ears to hear, let them hear” — the parable closes with invitation and warning. It is an invitation because ears to hear are a gift that can be sought; it is a warning because not everyone who physically hears understands. Jesus as the Word made flesh (John 1:14) is himself the object of this hearing — to hear his word rightly is to receive him.
10: The Twelve and the wider circle ask about the parables when alone — proximity to Jesus is the decisive factor in understanding. The disciples do not understand automatically; they ask. The act of seeking Jesus for explanation is itself the model of discipleship: those who pursue Jesus receive illumination.
11: “The secret of the kingdom of God has been given to you” — mystērion is Paul’s later term for the hidden plan of God revealed in Christ (Ephesians 1:9, Colossians 1:27). Here Jesus identifies himself as the one in whom the kingdom’s secret is present; the secret is not information but a person. To be with Jesus is to have the mystery; to be without him is to remain outside however many parables one hears.
12: “So that they may be ever seeing but never perceiving” — Isaiah 6:9-10, the great hardening text, is quoted as the purpose of parabolic teaching. This is not predestination to damnation but a judgment that confirms the stance people have already taken: those who come to Jesus with open hearts see; those who approach with hostility are confirmed in their blindness.
13: “Don’t you understand this parable? How then will you understand any parable?” — Jesus challenges the disciples’ comprehension; this is the first of Mark’s repeated misunderstanding scenes (Mark 6:52; Mark 7:18; Mark 8:17, 21; Mark 9:32). Full understanding waits for the Spirit after the resurrection (John 16:13).
14: “The farmer sows the word” — the interpretive key. “The word” (logos) is Jesus’s proclamation (Mark 1:45; Mark 2:2; Mark 4:33); in John, “the Word” is Jesus himself (John 1:1). Jesus is simultaneously the sower, the seed (the word embodied), and the field in which all things grow. The parable is a self-portrait.
15: Seed on the path — Satan immediately takes the word. The cosmic adversary introduced in the wilderness temptation (Mark 1:12-13) is now named as the opponent of the word’s reception. Every proclamation of the gospel is contested; the soil that does not respond allows the adversary to remove what was sown.
16: Rocky-ground hearers — receive the word with joy but have no root; “when trouble or persecution comes because of the word, they quickly fall away.” The Greek skandalizontai (they stumble) is the same word used for Peter’s impending fall (Mark 14:27, 29). Joy at Jesus’s presence does not guarantee endurance through his suffering.
17: “Since they have no root, they last only a short time” — rootlessness is the fundamental diagnosis. Root imagery in the OT describes Israel’s establishment in covenant (Isaiah 27:6; Jeremiah 17:8); to be rooted in Christ is to participate in the life he provides. These hearers have the surface but not the relationship.
18: Thorny-ground hearers — the word is sown but the worries of this life, the deceitfulness of wealth, and the desires for other things crowd in and choke it. The plurality of competing desires contrasts with the single-mindedness of the good-soil hearer (Mark 4:20). Undivided attention to the word requires willing dispossession — exactly what Jesus will call for in Mark 10:21.
19: “The deceitfulness of wealth” — wealth is “deceitful” (apatē) because it promises the security and sufficiency that belong to God alone. The disciples who have left everything (Mark 1:18, 20; Mark 2:14) have removed the thorns; the rich young man who will not (Mark 10:22) remains choked. The kingdom calls for a transfer of ultimate trust from possessions to the person of Jesus.
20: Good-soil hearers — “hear the word, accept it, and produce a crop.” The three-part description is the Christological response pattern: hear (receive the word addressed to you), accept (welcome it as authoritative), produce (the transformed life bears kingdom fruit — more disciples, more proclamation, the harvest multiplied).
21: “Do you bring in a lamp to put it under a bowl or a bed?” — the lamp is the revelation of the kingdom brought by Jesus; the Messianic Secret is temporary, not permanent. The light is hidden in the incarnation so that it may be revealed in the resurrection. What Jesus whispered in the days of his flesh will be proclaimed from the housetops.
22: “For whatever is hidden is meant to be disclosed, and whatever is concealed is meant to be brought out into the open” — the purpose of the hiddenness is its eventual disclosure. The resurrection is the great unveiling of all that was hidden in Jesus’s earthly ministry: his identity as Son of God, the meaning of his death, the nature of the kingdom.
23: “If anyone has ears to hear, let them hear” — the invitation of Mark 4:9 repeated, bracketing the lamp and measure sayings. The parable of the Sower, its interpretation, and these supplementary parables are all part of one teaching about how the word of the kingdom works. Hearing rightly is the through-line.
24: “Consider carefully what you hear. With the measure you use, it will be measured to you” — attentiveness to Jesus’s teaching is not passive; it is the active posture of faith. The measure of attention given to the word determines what is received back. Those who apply themselves to the word receive an abundance; those who give it partial attention receive correspondingly less.
25: “Whoever has will be given more; whoever does not have, even what they have will be taken from them” — the paradoxical economy of the kingdom: spiritual understanding grows when exercised and withers when neglected. Proximity to Jesus is maintained by seeking; the disciples who pursue Jesus (Mark 4:10) are given more; those who do not pursue lose even what they received.
26: The seed growing secretly — unique to Mark, this parable addresses the hiddenness of the kingdom’s growth between the first and second coming of Christ. Automate (by itself, automatically) points to God’s sovereign action in history that does not depend on human understanding. Jesus’s death and resurrection will look like failure; the harvest (Mark 4:29) will come on God’s timetable.
27: “Night and day, whether he sleeps or gets up, the seed sprouts and grows” — the continuity of the seed’s growth regardless of the farmer’s waking state emphasizes divine agency. Between the resurrection and the parousia, the kingdom grows through the proclaimed word while the Church both labors and rests; the outcome does not depend on perpetual human vigilance but on the word’s inherent vitality.
28: “All by itself the soil produces grain — first the stalk, then the head, then the full kernel in the head” — the stages of growth are the stages of redemptive history: the ministry of Jesus (stalk), the apostolic mission (head forming), the full harvest at the end of the age (kernel). The process has an internal logic; the end is certain because the beginning has been planted by the sower.
29: “As soon as the grain is ripe, he puts the sickle to it, because the harvest has come” — the harvest image invokes Joel 3:13 (eschatological judgment and ingathering). Jesus is both the sower who plants the word and the lord of the harvest who will return to gather what has grown. The certainty of the harvest is the ground of confidence throughout the age of hiddenness.
30: “What shall we say the kingdom of God is like?” — Jesus’s self-questioning before the mustard seed parable signals that human language approaches the kingdom’s reality obliquely. The act of seeking the right parable models the proper intellectual posture before the mystery of God’s reign: continual, humble searching.
31: “It is like a mustard seed, which is the smallest of all seeds on earth” — the smallness is the point: the kingdom’s visible beginning in a single man from Nazareth, executed under Roman authority, is as unpromising as a mustard seed. The incarnation chooses weakness and obscurity; the cross looks like the smallest, most insignificant end. This is the way the kingdom comes.
32: “Yet when planted, it grows and becomes the largest of all garden plants” — the eschatological reversal. “The birds of the air perch in its branches” echoes Ezekiel 17:23 and Daniel 4:12, where a great tree sheltering birds represents an empire encompassing all peoples. Jesus’s kingdom surpasses all earthly empires by the humility of its origin and the universality of its hospitality.
33: “With many such parables Jesus spoke the word to them, as much as they could understand” — the parables are measured to the capacity of the hearers; Jesus does not overwhelm but gradually opens the mystery as disciples grow. The progressive disclosure model of the parables mirrors the progressive revelation of Scripture: the OT types contained what the NT proclamation unfolds.
34: “He did not say anything to them without using a parable. But when he was alone with his disciples, he explained everything” — the two-tier teaching maps onto the structure of revelation: the parables are the OT types and shadows; the private explanation to the disciples is the NT fulfillment. Jesus alone has the authority to explain the mysteries of the kingdom because he is both their content and their interpreter.
35: “Let us go over to the other side” — the crossing to the Gentile side of the Sea of Galilee initiates the kingdom’s expansion beyond Israel. The “other side” (eis to peran) is used six times in Mark for Gentile territory. Jesus actively crosses the ethnic and geographic boundary that defines Israel’s self-understanding; the kingdom is not bounded by Israel’s borders.
36: “They took him along, just as he was, in the boat” — the “just as he was” detail is unique to Mark and emphasizes the genuine humanity of Jesus: not a prepared or protected figure but the weary man who had been teaching all day. The incarnation means vulnerability; the one who will still the storm is truly exhausted before doing so.
37: A furious squall — lailaps (squall/whirlwind) is a technical term for a severe storm arising suddenly; the Sea of Galilee is prone to such storms. The Jonah parallel is structural: a man asleep in a storm-tossed boat, with terrified companions who wake him (Jonah 1:4-6). Jesus will still the storm where Jonah could not — and at the cross, unlike Jonah, he will give his life willingly rather than be sacrificed.
38: Jesus asleep in the stern — the cushion detail (unique to Mark) emphasizes the physical reality of his exhaustion. The disciples’ desperate question — “Teacher, don’t you care if we drown?” — echoes Psalms 44:23 (“Rouse yourself! Why do you sleep, Lord?”) and invokes the covenant language of divine watchfulness from the one they have not yet identified as the Lord who neither slumbers nor sleeps (Psalms 121:4).
40: “Why are you so afraid? Do you still have no faith?” — Jesus rebukes the disciples after stilling the storm. The rebuke is pedagogical: the disciples’ fear and lack of faith are the exact failures that the resurrection will address. To have faith in Jesus as Lord of creation is the proper response to his presence; the disciples are not yet capable of this because they do not yet know who he is.
41: “Who is this? Even the wind and the waves obey him!” — the central Christological question of the Gospel. Psalms 89:9 (“You rule over the surging sea; when its waves mount up, you still them”) and Psalms 107:28-30 (God stills the storm) are the OT backdrop; Jesus exercises the authority Scripture attributes to YHWH alone. The answer to “Who is this?” is given finally by the centurion at the cross (Mark 15:39): Son of God.
